section.title: Spherical coordinates in integrals
section.p: Cylindrical coordinates made cans, pipes, and cones look simple. A ball is still awkward in cylindrical coordinates, because the top and bottom surfaces involve z=\pm\sqrt{a^2-r^2}. A ball wants a coordinate that measures distance from its center.
That coordinate is \rho. To locate a point in space, distance alone is not enough. We also need two angles.

subsection.title: The volume element \rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta
subsection.p: Spherical coordinates use \rho,\qquad \phi,\qquad \theta. The convention in this book is as follows.

table.title: The spherical coordinates and their meanings.

row.cell: angle around the z-axis, measured in the xy-plane
angle down from the positive z-axis

subsection.p: Thus 0\le \rho, \qquad 0\le \phi\le \pi. The coordinate \theta usually runs through an interval of length 2\pi.
The conversion formulas are \boxed{x=\rho\sin\phi\cos\theta, \qquad y=\rho\sin\phi\sin\theta, \qquad z=\rho\cos\phi.} The distance from the z-axis is r=\sqrt{x^2+y^2}=\rho\sin\phi.

figure.caption: The spherical volume element is bounded by small changes in \rho, \theta, and \phi.

subsection.p: Now find the small volume element.

A small spherical coordinate box has three side lengths, approximately: d\rho in the radial direction, \rho\,d\phi in the direction that changes \phi, and \rho\sin\phi\,d\theta in the direction that goes around the z-axis.
Multiplying these gives dV = d\rho\cdot \rho\,d\phi\cdot \rho\sin\phi\,d\theta. So \boxed{dV=\rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta.}

The factor \rho^2 says that shells farther from the origin are larger. The factor \sin\phi says that angular pieces shrink near the poles. Near \phi=0, all values of \theta crowd around the positive z-axis.
In form language, the same scale factor is \boxed{dx\wedge dy\wedge dz = \rho^2\sin\phi\, d\rho\wedge d\phi\wedge d\theta.} For positive volume, we write dV=\rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta.

statement.p:
Let B be the ball of radius 4 centered at the origin: x^2+y^2+z^2\le16. In spherical coordinates, this is simply 0\le\rho\le4,\qquad 0\le\phi\le\pi,\qquad 0\le\theta\le2\pi. Therefore
\operatorname{Volume}(B) \amp= \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^\pi\int_0^4 \rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta \amp= \left(\int_0^{2\pi}d\theta\right)\left(\int_0^\pi\sin\phi\,d\phi\right)\left(\int_0^4\rho^2\,d\rho\right) \amp= (2\pi)(2)\left(\frac{64}{3}\right) \amp= \frac{256\pi}{3}.
This matches \frac43\pi(4)^3=\frac{256\pi}{3}.

subsection.p: Again, the scale factor is not optional. If we computed the unit ball without \sin\phi, we would get \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^\pi\int_0^1 \rho^2\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta = \frac{2\pi^2}{3}, not \frac{4\pi}{3}. The missing \sin\phi would give too much weight to tiny angular pieces near the poles.

subsection.title: Balls, shells, and cones
subsection.p: Spherical coordinates turn spheres into constant-\rho surfaces: x^2+y^2+z^2=a^2 \qquad\Longleftrightarrow\qquad \rho=a. So balls and shells become very simple.

statement.p:
The shell between radius 2 and radius 5 is 2\le\rho\le5. With full angular range, 0\le\phi\le\pi,\qquad 0\le\theta\le2\pi. Its volume is
V \amp= \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^\pi\int_2^5 \rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta \amp= (2\pi)(2)\left[\frac{\rho^3}{3}\right]_2^5 \amp= 4\pi\cdot\frac{125-8}{3} \amp= 156\pi.

subsection.p: Cones also become simple, because a cone with vertex at the origin is a constant-angle surface. For example, z=\sqrt{x^2+y^2} becomes \rho\cos\phi=\rho\sin\phi. Away from the origin, this gives \cos\phi=\sin\phi, so \phi=\frac{\pi}{4}. The inside of the upper cone is 0\le\phi\le\frac{\pi}{4}.

example.title: A ball cut by a cone

subsection.p: A sphere fixes \rho. A cone fixes \phi. A vertical half-plane fixes \theta. That is the basic geometry of spherical bounds.

subsection.title: Choosing the angular bounds
subsection.p: The angle \theta is the same azimuth angle used in cylindrical coordinates. It describes the shadow in the xy-plane.
The angle \phi describes how far down from the positive z-axis the point lies. It controls vertical opening.

example.title: The first octant of a ball

statement.p:
The first octant means x\ge0,\qquad y\ge0,\qquad z\ge0. For the ball \rho\le4, the bounds are 0\le\rho\le4, \qquad 0\le\phi\le\frac{\pi}{2}, \qquad 0\le\theta\le\frac{\pi}{2}. The volume is
V \amp= \int_0^{\pi/2}\int_0^{\pi/2}\int_0^4 \rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta \amp= \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)(1)\left(\frac{64}{3}\right) \amp= \frac{32\pi}{3}.
This is one eighth of the full ball volume \frac{256\pi}{3}.

subsection.p: Spherical coordinates can also describe caps, where the radial lower bound is not zero.

statement.p:
Find the volume inside the sphere \rho\le3 and above the plane z=1. The plane becomes \rho\cos\phi=1. Along a ray with angle \phi, the plane is reached at \rho=\sec\phi. For the ray to reach the sphere after crossing the plane, we need \sec\phi\le3, or \cos\phi\ge\frac13. Thus 0\le\phi\le\arccos\frac13. The bounds are 0\le\theta\le2\pi, \qquad 0\le\phi\le\arccos\frac13, \qquad \sec\phi\le\rho\le3. So \boxed{V= \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\arccos(1/3)} \int_{\sec\phi}^{3} \rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta.} Compute:
V \amp= 2\pi\int_0^{\arccos(1/3)} \left[\frac{\rho^3}{3}\right]_{\sec\phi}^3 \sin\phi\,d\phi \amp= 2\pi\int_0^{\arccos(1/3)} \left(9-\frac13\sec^3\phi\right)\sin\phi\,d\phi.
Let \alpha=\arccos\frac13. Then \cos\alpha=\frac13, \qquad \tan^2\alpha=8. Also, \int_0^\alpha 9\sin\phi\,d\phi = 9(1-\cos\alpha)=6, and \frac13\int_0^\alpha \sec^3\phi\sin\phi\,d\phi = \frac13\int_0^\alpha \tan\phi\,\sec^2\phi\,d\phi = \frac16\tan^2\alpha = \frac43. Therefore V=2\pi\left(6-\frac43\right)=\frac{28\pi}{3}.

subsection.p: The angular bounds come from looking along rays from the origin. A ray either enters the solid immediately, enters after crossing a surface, or misses the solid. Spherical coordinates make us answer that ray question.

subsection.title: Applications to gravitational and radial densities
subsection.p:
Many three-dimensional densities depend only on distance from a central point. A planet model, a gas cloud, and a radially symmetric charge distribution all have this shape: \delta=\delta(\rho). For a ball of radius a, the total mass is
M \amp= \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^\pi\int_0^a \delta(\rho)\rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta \amp= 4\pi\int_0^a \delta(\rho)\rho^2\,d\rho.
So \boxed{M=4\pi\int_0^a \delta(\rho)\rho^2\,d\rho.} The angular part contributes 4\pi, and the radial part carries the real information.

The same scale factor explains a familiar feature of inverse-square laws. Suppose an energy density in a spherical shell is u(\rho)=\frac{10}{\rho^2}, \qquad 1\le\rho\le4. The total energy in the shell is
E \amp= \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^\pi\int_1^4 \frac{10}{\rho^2}\rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta \amp= 10(2\pi)(2)\int_1^4 d\rho \amp= 120\pi.
The \rho^2 in the volume element cancels the 1/\rho^2 in the density. Each unit of radial thickness contributes the same amount. Later, when we study flux, the same geometric growth of spherical area will help explain why gravitational and electric fields often have inverse-square form.

Cylindrical and spherical coordinates changed the shape of dV, but not the meaning of the integral. A triple integral still adds tiny contributions through a solid. Once we can add density, the next questions are natural: Where is the mass balanced? How far is it spread from an axis? How much charge or energy does the solid contain? Those are the applications that turn triple integrals from volume machines into physical measuring tools.
